text: A security check from "Microsoft" wanted me to give him the access to my computer to get rid of some malware. The person had a strong Indian/Asian accent, was very forceful and even downright rude trying to press me to follow his instructions. I was supposed to immediately install a remote access in his favour, at which point I sent him to blazes (the scam was at that moment too obvious). Anyone else would have complied as the person was very insistent. Quite a cunning scanner. As mentioned before, Microsoft (or another tech company) would NEVER CALL DIRECTLY ITS CUSTOMER.

Call from a French speaking person telling me that my computer was sending her messages.Followed by other calls later on from the Police and from hidden phone number.My phone number : 0799384726 The malicious caller's number:+41 78 729 45 56
Caller said he was from Microsoft, about a problem with my computer. I asked why he thought I was that stupid to believe that crap and he said "because you are...", confirming it was a scam.

Hello,The scammer used the following number: +353 84 166 13 38Pretending to call from a Microsoft Call center Best regards

Hi,
I guy with an Indian accent called me and he claimed to call from Microsoft. 
He was explaining that they have noticed based on "computer ID" or something that my Windows laptop had been hacked and that the hackers had accessed it several times over the past few days. 
I listened for a while, but then I told him that I am a professional and asked him to get another not a scammer job and that scamming people is not nice thing to do. I also told that I will report his call to the authorities (happening now). 

The call was coming from the number +41 79 8744445, but obviously, that is not the correct number. 

It would be interesting to know how much these calls come here in Switzerland and how much the operators and officials can do to stop these.
I got a call from +41 78 933 96 98 offering me support for my Windows computer, according to the caller my windows got hacked - I don’t use windows

I got a call from a woman saying that my computer had been hacked and it was sending out warning signals. They said that they would help me step by step to stop my computer from sending out these warning messages. The number that called me was 0792102053

I received a call today from +44.1268.709.559 (my phone identified the call as originating from "Basildon, England, United Kingdom.") The caller explained he was from "Windows Security Division" and that there had been a lot of bad traffic originating from my computer (which is an iMac by the way). I told him I was going to report the number to the Swiss government, which I am doing now. As soon as I hung up I got another call, this time from Italy, from +39.346.249.8227. I assumed this was the same character, just changing his dialling info, so I did not answer the phone. I have since blocked both numbers on my device. Thank you -- John Smith

I received a phone call at 19.31 on Thursday 16th August from +41 44 824 7111.  The caller claimed to be calling from Swisscom and that they have complaints from the banks about spam emails coming from my ip address.  They wanted to arrange an appointment tomorrow to help diagnose the issue on my computer.  I thanked them and said I would run my own scans and contact swisscom myself.

I was browsing the internet when a pop-up appeared saying my computer was infected and I had to call Microsoft immediately at 0800 123 456. I called and a man with a foreign accent asked me to install a remote access tool called AnyDesk. He then showed me my "infected" system files and asked for 250 CHF to clean the computer. I paid by credit card before realizing it was a scam.

I was called through a Milan phone number (+39 02 3952 9067) claiming to be from Microsoft and claiming someone has keylogged my computer. I disconnected the call after they tried to get my to open anydesk to enable them to hack my computer
Microsoft scam caller, trying to gain access to my laptop. Very aggressive and with ‘Indian’ accent. Had details of my name and MS license..

My elderly mother received a phone call from someone claiming to be from Swisscom technical support. They told her that her internet connection had been used for illegal activities and that she needed to pay 500 CHF in Neosurf vouchers to avoid prosecution. She bought the vouchers and gave them the codes before my brother intervened. The caller had a Swiss-German accent and knew her first name.

Scammers using a VOiP Nummber calling from India with an Italian nummber, claiming that they are Microsoft. Tried to get an Remote Accsess to my Computer. Called 2 times, every time with an Different nummbers and they deactivated the nummber imidiatley after the call.

The caller identified himself as a Microsoft Support representative. He spoke in English and it was obvious that he was Indian because of his accent. Call occurred on August 26, 2024 09:02 local time.

The website prompted the user to call this number: 0445860339 According to the website, it's the number of the Swiss Microsoft Support. The website displays a message that says: 
Starten sie Ihre PC nicht neu. Der Computer is deaktiviert. Bitte rufen sie den Support an. 
The website also automatically sets the browser into full screen, and displays multiple fake Window's popups warning about spyware.

Possible investment scam website targeting Swiss users

Misuse of personal data leading to harassment calls

Possible use of auto-dialing systems with spoofed numbers

+41 31 515 78 XX (endings 44, 94),

and others, calls usually come from slightly varying numbers.

Action I have taken:

The caller asked whether I was Mr. Bosch and inquired if I had initiated a CHF 500 transaction via TWINT. When I replied that I do not use TWINT, the caller further asked if I would like to cancel the transaction. I informed them that they could proceed as they wished and stated that I suspected the call to be fraudulent. I then terminated the call.

I respectfully request that the competent authorities investigate the aforementioned phone number and take the necessary measures to identify and hold the individual responsible accountable under the law. I remain at your disposal to provide any further information, either as a witness or as a potential victim of this attempted fraud.

Sincerely,
L. Rostov

Astec Boat SA (Switzerland) was involved with a cyberattack on the 23th of Jun 2013 at 0050CET, targeting Astec internal shared network involving Blackout Ransomware. The ransomware attacked affected multiple IT systems, including servers hosting sensitive customer data, financial records, and employee information. The compromised systems processed critical business information. The cyberattack has been resolved and Astec Boat SA has returned to normal operations.

Dear Madam / Sir,
I have just tried to report this through the online form but an error occurred.

I am working in the Modal Assurance team (Risk Management / Security) of flammèche Cosmetics, based in Basel.
During last weekend (31st march / 1st April), 1 database of our Group hosted by Microsoft Azure and containing personal data of customers has been attacked.
The adversary then accessed our mailing system (Sendgrid) and sent an email to some approximately 2,700 of our colleagues, in several waves yesterday and today. The emails were to inform of the attack, and to threaten to sell the stolen data if the company did not pay 45 BTC in ransom. You can find the emails in attachment.
We have established a crisis management team comprising of our internal IT teams in Switzerland and Czech Republic, our IT partners IBM and Microsoft, our Modal Assurance team in Switzerland, our Legal team, our CFO and CEO.

The first elements that we have collected indicate that the attacker compromised one of our web servers via trojans and got access to all data stored there, including credentials such as connection strings to storage accounts where we store documents containing personal data of flammèche Cosmetics customers. We are also able to confirm that the data was indeed downloaded by the adversary and then deleted from the database (approximately 4 TB).
Initial elements seem to indicate that the adversary is located in Russia and has been accessing a server and database primarily dedicated to the operation of our Russia and other ex-Soviet commercial markets.
We have taken immediate measures to limit any further damage. However at this stage we are not able to confirm whether the adversary is still in our infrastructure or not. We are currently informing relevant data protection authorities.
Please note that the data stolen does not concern – as far as we can tell – any Swiss data subjects, as our Group does not run any commercial operations in Switzerland.

Thank you and best regards,
Daniel Ratliff

Our production facility in Schaffhausen experienced a ransomware incident affecting our OT network. Several HMI workstations running Windows 7 were encrypted overnight. Production lines had to be stopped for 36 hours. The ransomware note references the LockBit group and demands payment in Monero. Our IT/OT segmentation was not properly enforced, which likely allowed lateral movement from the corporate network.

Subject: Cyber Attach concerning Ransomware (cyber incident impliquant un rançongiciel)
On January 12, 2020, the Local Chamber of Lengnau IT and communications systems have been hacked by a ransomware group, called Rhysida. The group hacked our servers located in Torino, Ticino, Switzerland and asked for payment (see attached files). At the same time, they threatened to share confidential material worldwide and compromise all our systems. Our IT team is now working together with an expert IT company (Counterhack)  to restore, clean, and put back on-line our servers; it may take several weeks to be back in full operation. I am writing using my personal email address (clarisham@stockpal.com) because our active directory, email,/data servers, have been compromised. Our ERP system (Microsoft Business Central) looks safe as it has been migrated in the cloud few months ago and we took further precautions after the attach. We remain at your disposal for any question. Clarissa hamberg, Local Chamber of Lengnau Director, Finance, IT & Facilities

TDD recently became aware of an IT security incident that impacted certain TDD systems.  TDD started an investigation, retained leading experts, notified certain law enforcement and data protection authorities, and implemented measures to contain and assess the incident.  The incident has now been successfully contained.  

All of TDD key services and systems are up and running, all factories are operating, and the company continues to serve its customers.  The company also continues to restore any remaining impacted services and systems and is further enhancing the security of its systems.

TDD has determined that an unauthorized third-party accessed certain TDD systems, deployed a type of ransomware that is not self-propagating, and exfiltrated certain data.  The company is working to identify and analyze the nature and scope of affected data, and is further assessing its notification obligations.  TDD will communicate with affected parties where necessary, including, for example, specific customers and/or individuals where personally identifiable information was affected.

We are a manufacturing company based in canton Aargau. On the night of 4 April, the AKIRA ransomware group encrypted our file server and three workstations. In addition to encryption, we have confirmed data exfiltration: a directory listing of our stolen files has already appeared on the AKIRA leak site. We have engaged an external incident response firm and have notified our cyber insurance provider. Estimated recovery time is 2 weeks.

We are a medical practice in Geneva with approximately 3,200 patient records. On the morning of 14 March, staff could no longer access our patient management system. All files on the local server were encrypted with a .MEOW extension. A ransom demand of 2 BTC was left in a text file. We have contacted our software vendor and our data protection officer. We believe the attack vector was RDP exposed to the internet.

We are a small accounting firm (8 employees) based in Zurich. Last Monday we noticed that several files on our shared drive had been renamed with a .ELBIE extension and could no longer be opened. We found a ransom note (HOW_TO_RESTORE.txt) on the desktop of one workstation demanding 0.5 BTC. We believe the initial access was via a phishing email opened by one of our employees three days earlier. Our IT provider has isolated the affected machine. No backup solution was in place.
